attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about her mood. She reports experiencing frequent mood swings for the past three years, alternating between periods of increased energy and productivity lasting a few days, followed by periods of low mood and decreased motivation lasting a similar duration. These mood changes occur multiple times throughout the year. She has never had severe depressive symptoms or manic episodes. Her symptoms have caused some difficulties in her relationships and work performance. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the clinical presentation, the most likely diagnosis for this patient is cyclothymic disorder. This condition is characterized by numerous periods of hypomanic symptoms alternating with periods of depressive symptoms that do not meet full criteria for hypomania or major depressive episode, respectively. The symptoms have persisted for at least two years, which is a key diagnostic criterion for cyclothymic disorder in adults.

Major depressive disorder is unlikely in this case because the patient reports alternating periods of increased energy and productivity, which are not consistent with major depression. Additionally, she has not experienced severe depressive symptoms that would meet the criteria for a major depressive episode.

Bipolar I disorder is characterized by the occurrence of one or more manic episodes, which are not present in this patient's history. The patient's symptoms are milder and more chronic than what would be expected in Bipolar I disorder.

Bipolar II disorder requires the presence of at least one hypomanic episode and one major depressive episode. While this patient experiences mood fluctuations, her symptoms do not meet the full criteria for either hypomania or major depression, making Bipolar II disorder less likely.

Borderline personality disorder can present with mood instability, but it typically involves more intense and short-lived mood changes, often triggered by interpersonal stressors. The chronic nature of this patient's symptoms and the absence of other typical borderline personality traits make this diagnosis less likely.

It's important to note that cyclothymic disorder can be a precursor to bipolar disorder in some cases, and careful follow-up is necessary. The diagnosis of cyclothymic disorder requires that the symptoms cause significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning, which is evident in this patient's case. Management typically involves psychoeducation, psychotherapy, and in some cases, mood-stabilizing medications.
learningPoints: Cyclothymic disorder is characterized by numerous periods of hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full criteria for hypomania or major depressive episode.
The symptoms of cyclothymic disorder must persist for at least two years (one year in children and adolescents) to meet diagnostic criteria.
Cyclothymic disorder is distinguished from bipolar disorder by the absence of full manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes.
references: American Psychiatric Association DS, American Psychiatric Association DS. Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders: DSM-5. Washington, DC: American psychiatric association; 2013 May 22.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
